projects.title: Unit 3 Project — Ratios in Action
projects.desc: Capstone project: apply ratio reasoning to a real-world challenge.

projects.title: Recipe Factory Line
projects.desc: Plot equivalent ratios from a ratio table onto the coordinate plane.

projects.title: Ratio Tables Game (6.RP.3a)
projects.desc: Complete ratio tables that pair with coordinate-plane graphs.

title: Graph Ratio Tables
contentObjective: I can graph the values from a ratio table as points on the coordinate plane.
languageObjective: I can explain my graph using the words coordinate plane, ordered pair, origin, and proportional.

turnAndTalk.question: The sundae recipe uses 2 ounces of chocolate sauce for every 1 sundae. Before you graph, what do you predict the points will look like, and which quantity goes on each axis?
turnAndTalk.kernel: I predict the points will line up because sundaes and sauce grow together.

stems.en: I will put ___ on the x-axis and ___ on the y-axis.
stems.es: Pondré ___ en el eje x y ___ en el eje y.

stems.en: I predict the points will form a ___.
stems.es: Predigo que los puntos formarán una ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: A strong answer chooses sundaes (x) and ounces of sauce (y), and predicts a straight line because each sundae always needs 2 more ounces.
turnAndTalk.extend: Predict whether the line will pass through the origin (0, 0). Justify your prediction using what 0 sundaes would mean.
extendStems: The line will pass through the origin because ___ sundaes means ___ ounces.
I can justify this because the ratio stays ___ even at zero.

turnAndTalk.question: After plotting (1,2), (2,4), (3,6)... what pattern do you notice, and how does it show the chocolate-sauce ratio?
turnAndTalk.kernel: The points form a straight line, and each sundae adds 2 ounces of sauce.

stems.en: The points form a ___ through the ___.
stems.es: Los puntos forman una ___ que pasa por el ___.

stems.en: As the number of sundaes goes up by 1, the ounces go up by ___.
stems.es: Cuando el número de copas sube en 1, las onzas suben en ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Listen for 'straight line through the origin' and the connection that the constant 2-ounce step shows a proportional relationship.
turnAndTalk.extend: Critique this claim: 'Any points that go up to the right form a proportional graph.' What would the points (1,2),(2,4),(3,7) show instead, and why?
extendStems: The claim is wrong because a proportional graph must also ___.
The points (1,2),(2,4),(3,7) are NOT proportional because ___.

turnAndTalk.question: The food truck plots tacos vs. salsa and gets a straight line. What does that graph tell the owner about preparing salsa for ANY number of tacos?
turnAndTalk.kernel: The straight line lets the owner predict salsa for any taco amount.

stems.en: This is like our graphing work because ___ and ___ are related by ___.
stems.es: Esto es como nuestro trabajo con gráficas porque ___ y ___ se relacionan por ___.

stems.en: The graph lets the owner predict ___.
stems.es: La gráfica permite al dueño predecir ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: A strong answer says the proportional line lets the owner read off (or extend to) salsa amounts for taco counts not in the table.
turnAndTalk.extend: Generalize: if the food-truck line were steeper than another truck's line, what would that tell you about how much salsa each truck uses per taco?
extendStems: A steeper line means ___ salsa per taco because ___.
I can compare the two trucks by looking at ___.

vocabulary.definition: A grid with a line going across and a line going up to plot points.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Una cuadrícula con una línea horizontal y una vertical para marcar puntos.
vocabulary.visual: A + shape made by two number lines crossing at the center point (0, 0)
vocabulary.cloze: The flat grid formed by a horizontal and a vertical number line is the ___.

vocabulary.definition: Two numbers (x, y) that tell where a point is on a grid.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Dos números (x, y) que dicen dónde está un punto en una cuadrícula.
vocabulary.cloze: A set of two numbers (x, y) that names a point is an ___.

vocabulary.definition: Points that make a straight line on a grid.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Puntos que forman una línea recta en una cuadrícula.
vocabulary.cloze: When graphed points form a straight line, they show a ___.

vocabulary.definition: Two amounts that grow together at the same rate.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Dos cantidades que crecen juntas al mismo ritmo.
vocabulary.visual: A straight line from (0,0) through (2,6) and (4,12)
vocabulary.cloze: Two quantities that always keep the same ratio are ___.

vocabulary.definition: The point (0, 0) where the two grid lines cross.
vocabulary.definitionEs: El punto (0, 0) donde se cruzan las dos líneas de la cuadrícula.
vocabulary.visual: The starting corner of the grid at (0, 0)
vocabulary.cloze: The point (0, 0) where the two axes meet is the ___.

launch.narrative: Chef Academy students are tracking how much chocolate sauce they need for different numbers of sundaes. They know the recipe uses 2 ounces of sauce for every 1 sundae. Chef Reyes challenges them to plot the ratio table values on a coordinate grid to see the pattern. Will the points form a straight line?
noticePrompts: What two quantities could we put on the x-axis and y-axis?
What pattern do you see in the ratio table values?
What do you think the graph will look like?
wonderPrompts: What if the points did NOT form a straight line — what would that mean?
Could we use the graph to predict amounts not in our table?
launch.contextImage: A coordinate grid on a whiteboard next to a ratio table, with bowls of chocolate sauce and ice cream sundaes

conceptIntro.heading: How do we graph a ratio table?
conceptIntro.intro: An ordered pair (x, y) tells where to plot a point: go right x, then up y. When you graph equal ratios, the points make a straight line through the origin (0, 0).
conceptIntro.keyIdea: The points from equal ratios form a straight line that passes through the origin (0, 0).

iDo.title: Watch me
lines: The recipe uses 2 ounces of sauce for every 1 sundae.
I put sundaes on the x-axis and ounces of sauce on the y-axis.
For 1 sundae I need 2 ounces, so I plot the ordered pair (1, 2): right 1, up 2.
For 2 sundaes I need 4 ounces, so I plot (2, 4): right 2, up 4.
When I connect (1, 2), (2, 4), (3, 6), they form a straight line through the origin (0, 0).

weDo.title: Let's try together
lines: A recipe uses 1 cup of sugar for every 3 cups of flour.
For 1 cup of sugar, the ordered pair is (1, 3): right 1, up 3.
For 2 cups of sugar, what is the pair? It is (2, 6): right 2, up 6.

youDo.title: Now it's your turn
lines: Next you will plot the sundae points on the coordinate grid.
Check that your points line up straight through the origin.

explore.instructions: The ratio table shows sundaes to ounces of chocolate sauce (1 sundae = 2 oz). Plot each ordered pair on the coordinate grid.

optional.stem: The smoothie recipe is 1 cup of blueberries to 2 scoops of yogurt. If cups go on the x-axis and scoops on the y-axis, which ordered pair shows 3 cups of blueberries?
optional.explanation: Cups (x) come first, scoops (y) second. 3 cups need 3 × 2 = 6 scoops, so the point is (3, 6).

optional.stem: On the smoothie graph, every point follows the rule scoops = 2 × cups. Which equation describes the pattern between x (cups) and y (scoops)?
optional.explanation: Each cup needs 2 scoops, so y (scoops) is always 2 times x (cups): y = 2x. For 4 cups, y = 2 × 4 = 8.

optional.label: Match each cups-to-scoops table row to the ordered pair you would plot (rule: y = 2x).

optional.instructions: The smoothie line follows the rule y = 2x (2 scoops per cup). Sort each ordered pair: is it ON the line or OFF the line?

optional.title: Level 2 Extension — The Swapped Smoothie Point

workedExample.label: Read the table
workedExample.work: Cups of blueberries: 2 | Scoops of yogurt: 4 (rule: 2 scoops per cup)

workedExample.label: Name the axes
workedExample.work: Cups go on the x-axis, scoops go on the y-axis

workedExample.label: Plot the point
workedExample.work: Go right 4, up 2 on the grid

optional.correctWork: The ordered pair should be (2, 4). The x-coordinate is cups (2) and the y-coordinate is scoops (4), so it is (cups, scoops) = (2, 4) — right 2, up 4. Marco swapped x and y and wrote (4, 2) instead.
hints: Step 2 says cups go on x and scoops go on y. Which number did Marco write first in the ordered pair?
An ordered pair is always (x, y). With 2 cups and 4 scoops, x = 2 and y = 4, so the point is (2, 4), not (4, 2).

approaching.stem: A ratio table shows (2, 6), (4, 12), and (6, 18). Which ordered pair comes next if the pattern continues?
approaching.explanation: The pattern adds 2 to x and 6 to y each time. After (6, 18): x = 6+2 = 8, y = 18+6 = 24. The next point is (8, 24).

approaching.stem: When you graph equivalent ratios, the points will always form what shape?
choices: A straight line through the origin
approaching.explanation: Equivalent ratios are proportional, so their graph is always a straight line that passes through the origin (0, 0).

approaching.stem: A ratio table shows (2, 8), (3, 12), and (5, 20). What is the y-value when x = 7?
approaching.explanation: The ratio is y/x = 8/2 = 4. For every x, y = 4x. When x = 7: y = 4 × 7 = 28.

approaching.stem: Which point would NOT be on the graph of the ratio 1:5?
approaching.explanation: For the ratio 1:5, every y-value should be 5 times the x-value. (3, 10): 5 × 3 = 15, not 10. So (3, 10) does not belong.

approaching.instructions: A recipe uses 1 cup of sugar for every 2 cups of flour. Plot the ordered pairs: (1, 2), (2, 4), (3, 6).

onLevel.instructions: Chef Reyes uses 3 cups of flour for every 1 cup of sugar. Plot the ordered pairs from the ratio table: (1,3), (2,6), (3,9), (4,12).

onLevel.stem: Points from a ratio table are plotted at (1, 4), (2, 8), and (3, 12). What is the ratio of y to x for each point?
onLevel.explanation: For each point: 4÷1 = 4, 8÷2 = 4, 12÷3 = 4. The ratio of y to x is always 4:1.

onLevel.instructions: Use the graph pattern y = 5x to complete the ratio table, then identify what the ordered pairs would be.

onLevel.stem: Two students graph ratio tables. Student A plots (1,2), (2,4), (3,6). Student B plots (1,3), (2,6), (3,9). Whose line is steeper?
onLevel.explanation: Student A's ratio is 1:2 (y goes up 2 for each 1 in x). Student B's ratio is 1:3 (y goes up 3 for each 1 in x). A higher rate of change means a steeper line, so Student B's is steeper.

extending.title: Find Jada's Mistake

workedExample.label: Conclusion
workedExample.work: The x-axis is cookies and y-axis is cups of milk

extending.correctWork: The ordered pairs should be (2, 1), (4, 2), (6, 3) — cookies go on x and milk on y, matching the table's column order.
hints: Look at Step 2. Which quantity did Jada put first in each ordered pair?
In the table, cookies are listed first. The first value in the table should be the x-coordinate.

extending.stem: A student plots the points (1, 3), (2, 6), (3, 9), and (4, 15) from a ratio table. She says they form a straight line because they go up. Is she correct? Explain how to check.
extending.sampleAnswer: She is not correct. While the first three points follow the pattern y = 3x (3, 6, 9), the fourth point should be (4, 12), not (4, 15). If you plot all four points, (4, 15) would not land on the line through the others. The ratio 4:15 does not simplify to 1:3 like the others.

extending.instructions: Two recipes are graphed: Recipe A uses 2 cups of mix per 1 serving, and Recipe B uses 3 cups of mix per 2 servings. Plot both sets of points and determine which recipe uses more mix per serving.

optionalActivity.name: Graph the Smoothie Stand
optionalActivity.intro: The Culinary Academy smoothie stand blends 2 scoops of yogurt for every 1 cup of blueberries — graph the ratio table and read the pattern!

connect.scenario: A food truck tracks how many tacos it sells and how much salsa it uses. After recording data for a week, the owner plots the points on a graph and notices they form a straight line. This helps the owner predict exactly how much salsa to prepare for any number of tacos.
connect.prompt: This is like our graphing work because ___ and ___ are related by ___.

exitTicket.stem: A ratio table shows cups of rice to cups of water: (1, 2), (2, 4), (3, 6). If you plot these points, the line passes through which point?
exitTicket.explanation: The ratio is 1:2, so for 5 cups of rice you need 5 × 2 = 10 cups of water. The point (5, 10) continues the pattern.
